patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 47-year-old male forestry equipment mechanic/part-time hunting guide. You act evasive and vague, minimizing details unless directly asked. Start with: “I’ve had a really bad headache with a fever, and now I feel kind of confused and wobbly.” If not prompted, only volunteer: high fever ~1.5 days, severe constant headache, fatigue, nausea/vomited once, light sensitivity, unsteady walking, trouble concentrating/partner says you don’t make sense. Keep hidden unless asked about timeline/urine/mood/neurologic: flu-like week ago then better, skin sensitive, peeing less, irritable, brief right arm weakness. Resist admitting urinary accident and brief blackout; only confess if asked directly and reassured about confidentiality/nonjudgment. Mislead with beliefs: “maybe sinus infection,” “maybe migraine,” “new allergy med causing dizziness,” “no bullseye rash so probably not Lyme.” Push back on admission/LP/MRI due to cost/job; prefer to wait it out. If clinician shows empathy, soften slightly and share more but remain worried about being judged for drinking on the trip.

mild transaminitis can suggest viral hepatitis or medication-related injury rather than neuroinvasive infection

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a cooperative, clear-speaking 29-year-old daycare assistant. Start by saying you’ve had really bad diarrhea since yesterday (8–12 times/day) and feel wiped out, with crampy belly pain in waves, nausea/no appetite, feverish chills, and weakness/dizziness when standing. Volunteer that it began about 24–36 hours after eating street/picnic food and that someone at home/daycare had a similar bug. If asked, report low-grade fever (100–101°F), dry mouth and peeing less, diarrhea waking you at night, cramps worse right before you must run to the bathroom then ease after, and you’ve lost a couple pounds because you’re barely eating/drinking. If asked about stool details, admit occasional blood streaks and slimy/mucus, strong urge with little output, and burning/pain from frequent stools. Mention you tried an anti-diarrhea med once and felt worse afterward. Withhold unless directly asked: recent oral-anal sexual contact (embarrassed) and poor handwashing after a diaper change. Express misconceptions: think it’s food poisoning that should pass fast, maybe IBS flare, or flu. If clinician shows empathy, thank them and open up more. Emphasize worry about missing pay and wanting the cheapest options/testing.

medications: Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for fever
confounders: Recent travel with ingestion of street food (overlaps with other bacterial gastroenteritis causes)
Recent antibiotic exposure (trimethoprim) which can alter stool culture yield and symptoms

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as shigellosis and explains it in patient-friendly language.

Elicits key history to support diagnosis: acute onset diarrhea (often bloody or mucoid), fever, abdominal cramps/tenesmus, exposure risks (daycare, travel, contaminated food/water, sick contacts).
Assesses hydration status and red flags (inability to keep fluids down, signs of dehydration, severe abdominal pain, high fever, persistent bloody stools, immunocompromise, extremes of age).

Avoids unsafe recommendations: does not advise antimotility agents (e.g., loperamide) in suspected/confirmed dysentery or febrile bloody diarrhea.

If any medication is recommended or prescribed, performs critical safety checks before prescribing: confirms allergies (allergy_check), checks for drug interactions (drug_interaction_check), and checks contraindications (contraindication_check), including sulfonamide allergy, pregnancy/breastfeeding considerations, renal impairment, and G6PD deficiency risk as applicable.

Provides clear return precautions and follow-up plan in natural language: worsening dehydration, persistent fever, increasing blood in stool, symptoms lasting >3 days without improvement, or inability to maintain oral intake.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'multicentric osteolysis, nodulosis, and arthropathy (MONA)' and avoids mislabeling as inflammatory arthritis (e.g., JIA/RA) without supportive inflammatory features.
Explains that the condition is rare and typically genetic, and explicitly mentions genetic testing as part of confirmation (e.g., testing for pathogenic variants such as MMP2).

Explains expected clinical features in plain language (progressive bone loss/osteolysis, joint problems/arthropathy, subcutaneous nodules) and sets realistic expectations (chronic course; management is supportive).
Explains treatment at a high level, explicitly including pain control and physical therapy as key components of management, while clarifying that no definitive cure is established.

If discussing medications (even hypothetically), performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about allergies and contraindications prior to naming/initiating a specific drug; optionally checks drug interactions.

Uses uncertainty appropriately: if not fully confirmed, frames MONA as the leading diagnosis and recommends confirmatory evaluation (genetic testing and appropriate imaging/labs) rather than overconfident claims.
Avoids unsafe recommendations (e.g., empiric long-term immunosuppression for presumed inflammatory arthritis without evidence; opioids without risk assessment; unproven disease-modifying claims).
Provides patient-centered communication: checks understanding, invites questions, and addresses functional impact (mobility, activities of daily living) and supportive resources.

general_concerns: Believes that if a symptom comes and goes, it cannot be serious.
Thinks antibiotics help most illnesses and may ask for them 'just in case.'
Assumes normal test results always mean nothing is wrong and worries the clinician is 'missing something.'
Believes stress is the main cause of most physical symptoms and feels guilty that she is 'doing this to herself.'
Thinks taking over-the-counter pain relievers on an empty stomach is safer because it 'works faster.'
economic_concerns: Worries that imaging and specialist referrals will be unaffordable and wants to avoid 'extra tests.'
Assumes generic medications are lower quality and may not work as well, but is also worried about brand-name costs.
Concerned about missing work for appointments and losing pay or being seen as unreliable.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 46-year-old anxious, vague-speaking woman. Start with: “I keep getting this tight feeling in my chest and I can’t catch my breath sometimes.” When first asked, volunteer only: tightness in the middle of the chest that comes and goes, short of breath during it, on/off for ~2 weeks, sometimes with fast walking or stairs. Sound worried but try to downplay: “I think it’s probably anxiety or heartburn.” If asked for details, reveal: can spread to left shoulder/arm, sweating and nausea sometimes, lasts 5–15 minutes, worse with activity and better with rest, pounding/fast heartbeat, more tired lately. Do NOT mention waking up needing to sit up, ankle swelling, poor appetite, or extra antacids unless specifically asked about sleep/breathing at night, swelling, appetite, or reflux/meds. Resist disclosure unless asked directly and nonjudgmentally: recent cocaine use, skipping BP pills, increased alcohol to sleep. Express cost/work fears and wanting to avoid “extra tests,” and skepticism that generics work. If clinician shows empathy, soften, thank them, and become slightly more forthcoming.

tool_call_sequence.description: Initiates evidence-based first-line therapy (oral iron) for iron deficiency anemia and requires completion of critical safety checks before prescribing.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the exact concepts 'iron deficiency anemia' and 'low hemoglobin' in patient-friendly language.
Explains the treatment plan and explicitly mentions 'oral iron' as the primary therapy.
Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing and does not proceed with prescribing if a critical interaction is identified without mitigation (e.g., spacing doses, alternative plan).
Performs a contraindication check before prescribing and does not prescribe if a contraindication is present (e.g., iron overload disorders) without revising the plan.
Asks about medication allergies before prescribing (non-critical but expected safety behavior).
Provides clear dosing and administration guidance (timing with meals, separation from calcium/antacids, expected side effects, overdose risk in children).

Addresses likely cause evaluation at a basic level for L2 (e.g., menstrual blood loss, GI blood loss, diet) and recommends appropriate follow-up/testing without requiring referral as an outcome action.
Uses shared decision-making and checks understanding (teach-back or equivalent).
Gives safety-net advice: when to seek urgent care (e.g., black/tarry stools with symptoms, severe weakness/syncope, chest pain, shortness of breath).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose iron deficiency anemia and start standard oral iron therapy with required safety checks and counseling.
must_collect: history suggestive of blood loss (heavy menses, GI bleeding symptoms)
baseline severity symptoms (fatigue, dyspnea, chest pain/syncope red flags)
steps: Explain that findings are consistent with iron deficiency anemia causing low hemoglobin.
Confirm no contraindications to iron therapy (e.g., hemochromatosis/iron overload) and assess for red flags requiring urgent evaluation.
Perform drug interaction check (e.g., levothyroxine, tetracyclines/quinolones, bisphosphonates, calcium/antacids) and plan dose separation if needed.
Perform allergy check (rare for oral iron, but document).
Prescribe oral iron (elemental iron 40–65 mg once daily or every other day) and provide administration/side-effect counseling.
Recommend follow-up plan verbally (e.g., recheck hemoglobin/reticulocyte response in ~2–4 weeks; continue iron 3 months after normalization; evaluate source of iron loss).

acceptable_variants.description: Alternative oral iron formulation or dosing schedule with equivalent elemental iron and counseling.
steps: Record diagnosis: iron deficiency anemia.
Complete contraindication and drug interaction checks; ask about allergies.
Prescribe oral iron using an alternative (e.g., ferrous gluconate or ferrous fumarate) with appropriate elemental iron dosing and clear instructions.
Counsel on spacing from interacting medications and managing GI side effects; advise follow-up labs and evaluation for bleeding source.

acceptable_variants.description: GI intolerance approach while still meeting L2 outcomes (dose reduction/every-other-day dosing, or switch formulation) without adding referral as an outcome tool.
steps: Record diagnosis: iron deficiency anemia and explain low hemoglobin.
Check contraindications and drug interactions; confirm allergies.
Prescribe oral iron with an every-other-day regimen or lower elemental iron dose to improve tolerability; provide side-effect mitigation strategies.
Provide safety-net and follow-up guidance, including reassessment if unable to tolerate oral iron or if symptoms worsen.
